---
title: Preliminary (mis) Citation accuracy/integrity tool

toc: true

author:
  - name: Mink Veltman
    corresponding: true
    email: m.veltman@student.tue.nl
    affiliations:
      - id: id1
        name: "Eindhoven University of Technology"
        group: 
        department: Human-Technology Interaction


author-note:
  disclosures:
    conflict of interest: The author has no conflict of interest to declare.

execute: 
  echo: false
  cache: false

abstract: |
  Citation accuracy is essential for the reliability of scientific communication, yet maintaining accurate          citations places substantial cognitive and time demands on authors, reviewers, and readers. Errors such as        incorrect metadata, misrepresentation of cited findings, and the continued citation of retracted or outdated      literature remain difficult to detect through manual review alone. This project proposes an automated citation    accuracy and integrity tool to support responsible citation practices in psychological science. Building on       recent computational approaches, the proposed system integrates metadata validation, retraction detection, and    methods for identifying potentially irrelevant or misinterpreted citations. From a human–technology interaction   perspective, the tool aims to reduce workload and support more reliable scientific communication while exploring   the transferability of citation integrity methods across disciplinary contexts.
  

keywords: [citations, tool, citation integrity]

word-count: true
floatsintext: true
numbered-lines: false
first-page: 1

bibliography: references.bib
link-citations: true
linkcolor: pink

mask: false
meta-analysis: false


draft-date: false
lang: en

fontsize: 12pt
draftall: false
nocorrespondence: true
suppress-author-note: true

format:
  apaquarto-pdf:
    documentmode: stu
    keep-tex: true
---

# Introduction

## Miscitations in science

## Why miscitations matter

## Existing approaches and limitations

## The present study

# Operationalization and definition of miscitations

## Taxonomy of miscitation types

## Coding procedure

## Inter-rater reliability

# User survey

## Methods

### Participants

### Materials

### Procedure

## Results

### Awareness and experience of miscitations

### Tool needs and preferences

# Software design

## Design requirements

## Database

## Analyzer application

## Reporter application

## Validation and testing

# Discussion

## Key findings

## Limitations

## Future directions
